source: Anthropic official frontend-design plugin (claude-plugins-official marketplace) — /home/devswha/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/claude-plugins-official/plugins/frontend-design/ (SKILL.md + README.md); upstream: github.com/anthropics/claude-code plugins

principles.name: named-tone-commitment
principles.rule: Before writing any markup, commit to exactly ONE named aesthetic pole from a tone menu — brutally minimal, maximalist chaos, retro-futuristic, organic/natural, luxury/refined, playful/toy-like, editorial/magazine, brutalist/raw, art deco/geometric, soft/pastel, industrial/utilitarian — and derive every CSS decision from it. Key phrase: 'intentionality, not intensity' — bold maximalism and refined minimalism both work; the middle does not.
principles.buildApplication: Add a 'tone' field (one value from the enumerated menu) to the concept sheet before Phase 3. Every font, color, spacing, and effect choice in the single HTML/CSS page must be justifiable against the named tone; when two options are equal, pick the one truer to the tone.
principles.evidence: SKILL.md 'Design Thinking' section (Tone bullet, CRITICAL paragraph)
principles.machineCheckable: Partially: a parser can verify the concept sheet contains a tone field whose value is one of the enumerated menu items. Whether the page honors the tone is LLM-lane.

principles.name: display-body-font-pairing
principles.rule: Pair one characterful display font (headings) with one refined body font (text) — two distinct families with distinct roles. Never set the whole page in a single generic family. Explicit denylist as primary font: Inter, Roboto, Arial, system font stacks; also avoid converging on trendy defaults (Space Grotesk named as the convergence example).
principles.buildApplication: Load exactly 2 font families (Google Fonts <link> or @font-face). Assign the display family to h1–h3 with deliberate weight/letter-spacing; the body family to p/li/small text. Choose the pairing from the named tone (e.g., editorial -> serif display + humanist sans body).
principles.evidence: SKILL.md 'Frontend Aesthetics Guidelines' Typography bullet + NEVER paragraph + anti-convergence paragraph
principles.machineCheckable: Yes: parse font-family declarations; require >= 2 distinct non-generic families with heading selectors using a different family than body selectors; fail if the heading font resolves to {Inter, Roboto, Arial, system-ui, -apple-system} (denylist extensible with Space Grotesk).

principles.name: anti-convergence-across-generations
principles.rule: No two generated designs should be the same: vary light vs dark themes, font choices, and aesthetic direction across generations. Never converge on a common default (Space Grotesk cited) run after run.
principles.buildApplication: Before building, scan previous session artifacts (e.g., root v0-*.html) for the display font and body background polarity used, and pick a different font family and/or theme polarity for the new page.
principles.evidence: SKILL.md paragraph: 'No design should be the same. Vary between light and dark themes... NEVER converge on common choices (Space Grotesk, for example) across generations.'
principles.machineCheckable: Yes with run history: diff the new page's heading font-family and body background luminance (light/dark) against the last N outputs; fail on exact repeat of the (font, polarity) pair.

principles.name: dominant-plus-sharp-accent-palette
principles.rule: Dominant colors with sharp accents outperform timid, evenly-distributed palettes. Structure the palette as: one dominant color family carrying the large surfaces, plus a small number of high-contrast accents reserved for emphasis — not 4–5 colors used in equal measure.
principles.buildApplication: Define the palette as CSS custom properties: one dominant surface family (background + section surfaces, covering the clear majority of page area), one sharp accent applied only to the CTA, links, and 1–2 key highlights. No second accent unless the tone demands it.
principles.evidence: SKILL.md Color & Theme bullet: 'Dominant colors with sharp accents outperform timid, evenly-distributed palettes.'
principles.machineCheckable: Partially: visual lane can compute an area-weighted color histogram of the rendered page and check that the top color family covers a supermajority of pixels (suggested threshold ~70%+) while accent-hue coverage stays small (suggested <10%). Thresholds are our operationalization, not from the source.

principles.name: css-variable-theme-tokens
principles.rule: Use CSS variables for color/theme consistency — the palette lives in custom properties, not scattered literals, so the aesthetic stays cohesive.
principles.buildApplication: Declare the full palette (surface, text, accent, border) as --tokens on :root of the single page; all color declarations reference var(--token). This also makes the dominant+accent structure auditable.
principles.evidence: SKILL.md Color & Theme bullet: 'Use CSS variables for consistency.'
principles.machineCheckable: Yes: parse CSS — :root defines >= 4 color custom properties and a majority share of color/background-color declarations use var() rather than raw literals.

principles.name: orchestrated-load-reveal
principles.rule: Concentrate the motion budget into high-impact moments: ONE well-orchestrated page-load sequence with staggered reveals via animation-delay creates more delight than scattered micro-interactions. Prefer CSS-only animation for plain HTML.
principles.buildApplication: Define 1 entrance @keyframes (e.g., fade+rise) and apply it to the hero's children with incrementing animation-delay steps (e.g., 0ms / 80ms / 160ms / 240ms). Do not animate every section; below-the-fold content stays static or uses at most subtle hover transitions. Zero JS required.
principles.evidence: SKILL.md Motion bullet: 'one well-orchestrated page load with staggered reveals (animation-delay)... Prioritize CSS-only solutions for HTML.'
principles.machineCheckable: Yes: CSS parse — at most 1–2 entrance @keyframes; animation-delay values on sibling selectors form a strictly increasing stagger; total count of load-animated selector groups under a small cap (suggested <= 6).

principles.name: surprising-hover-states
principles.rule: Hover states should surprise — interactive elements respond with more than a color swap (transform, reveal, shadow shift), with a transition so the change is felt.
principles.buildApplication: On the primary CTA and nav links of the static page, define :hover rules that change transform/box-shadow/underline geometry in addition to (or instead of) color, paired with a transition declaration on the base selector. Keep it to interactive elements only.
principles.evidence: SKILL.md Motion bullet: 'Use scroll-triggering and hover states that surprise.'
principles.machineCheckable: Yes: parse CSS — every <a>/button-like selector with a :hover rule must alter a property beyond color/background (transform, box-shadow, text-decoration geometry) and the base selector must declare transition.

principles.name: grid-breaking-composition
principles.rule: Spatial composition vocabulary: unexpected layouts — asymmetry, overlap, diagonal flow, grid-breaking elements. At least one deliberate move where an element escapes the column grid: overlapping a section boundary, rotated, or bleeding off the container.
principles.buildApplication: In the single page, implement one named grid-breaking technique: a hero image/blurb pulled across the section seam with negative margin, a transform: rotate(-2deg..-6deg) on one display element, or an element that overlaps a neighbor via grid placement. Exactly one to two such moves — it is a signature, not a system.
principles.evidence: SKILL.md Spatial Composition bullet: 'Unexpected layouts. Asymmetry. Overlap. Diagonal flow. Grid-breaking elements.'
principles.machineCheckable: Yes (visual lane): from rendered geometry, detect at least one pair of overlapping non-parent sibling boxes or one visible element with non-identity computed rotation/translation; static fallback: presence of negative margins or transform rotate in CSS.

principles.name: whitespace-polarity
principles.rule: Pick a spacing pole and commit: generous negative space OR controlled density. The undifferentiated middle (moderate padding everywhere) is the generic tell. The pole follows from the named tone.
principles.buildApplication: Airy pole: section vertical padding on the order of 96–144px, short line lengths, few elements per viewport. Dense pole: padding on the order of 32–48px, multi-column, information-rich. Apply one pole consistently for the page (a single deliberate counter-density section is allowed per L3's remedy). Numeric bands are our operationalization; the source names the pattern.
principles.evidence: SKILL.md Spatial Composition bullet: 'Generous negative space OR controlled density.'
principles.machineCheckable: Heuristic: visual lane can measure per-section content-box area / section area; flag pages whose density sits uniformly in a mid band (neither airy nor dense). Thresholds would need calibration on fixtures.

principles.name: atmospheric-backgrounds
principles.rule: Backgrounds create atmosphere and depth rather than defaulting to flat solid colors. Named techniques: gradient meshes, noise/grain textures, geometric patterns, layered transparencies, dramatic shadows, decorative borders, custom cursors. The technique must match the overall aesthetic, and (per our C1) stay out of the purple-gradient cliche.
principles.buildApplication: Give the hero or body at least one non-flat background layer built with pure CSS: an SVG data-URI noise overlay at low opacity, a repeating-linear-gradient geometric pattern, or a radial-gradient mesh in the palette's own hues (avoid hue 230–300 at sat >= 0.25, which trips the C1 machine check). Keep text contrast accessible over it.
principles.machineCheckable: Partially: static parse can verify body/hero background uses background-image / multiple background layers / pseudo-element texture rather than a single flat color, and that gradient stops avoid the C1 hue band. 'Matches the aesthetic' remains LLM-lane.

principles.name: dramatic-single-role-shadow
principles.rule: Shadows, when used, are dramatic and role-specific — a statement (e.g., hard offset block shadow in a brutalist tone, or one large glow on the single floating element) — never the same timid soft shadow sprinkled everywhere. This is the positive complement of C4.
principles.buildApplication: Either zero shadows, or one to two distinct box-shadow values: a hard-offset shadow like '6px 6px 0 <ink>' for brutalist/playful tones, or a single large soft shadow on exactly one floating element class. Never the default '0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,.1)' on every card.
principles.evidence: SKILL.md Backgrounds & Visual Details bullet ('dramatic shadows'); read against design-tells C4
principles.machineCheckable: Yes: parse CSS — count distinct box-shadow values (<= 2) and count selectors per value (the dramatic one appears on <= 1 element class). Complements the existing C4 uniform-shadow check rather than duplicating it.

principles.name: complexity-matches-vision
principles.rule: Match implementation complexity to the aesthetic vision: maximalist designs need elaborate code (extensive animations, layered effects); minimalist/refined designs need restraint, precision, and care in spacing, typography, and subtle details. Elegance comes from executing the vision well — not from adding more.
principles.buildApplication: Derive an explicit effect budget from the tone before building the page. Minimal tone: <= 1 animation, <= 2 shadows, flat or single-texture surfaces, effort goes into a strict spacing scale and type tuning. Maximal tone: layered backgrounds, coordinated multi-element animation, dense detail — fully committed, not half-applied.
principles.evidence: SKILL.md IMPORTANT paragraph: 'Match implementation complexity to the aesthetic vision.'
principles.machineCheckable: Partially: count effects in CSS (@keyframes, gradient layers, distinct shadows, filters) and compare against the tone declared in the concept sheet (minimal tones get a low cap). Requires the tone field from named-tone-commitment.

principles.name: one-memorable-differentiator
principles.rule: Decide up front what makes the page UNFORGETTABLE — the one thing someone will remember — and execute that single signature element fully while everything else stays quiet in support.
principles.buildApplication: Designate one signature move for the page (an extreme display-type treatment in the hero, a distinctive background system, or the grid-breaking element) and give it the largest visual budget; all other sections use the restrained baseline so the signature reads clearly.

notes: OVERLAPS with core/design-tells.md (do not re-add): the skill's NEVER list ('purple gradients on white backgrounds') == C1; 'predictable layouts and component patterns' == L1/L3/L4; 'cookie-cutter design' is the general thesis behind the whole tell list; its asymmetry/overlap prescription is the positive mirror of S3's remedy ('의도적 비대칭'); its anti-generic-font stance has NO counterpart in design-tells — there is currently no font-family tell at all, making display-body-font-pairing a promotable static machine check. CONFLICTS/TENSIONS: (1) Skill prescribes 'layered transparencies' as a background technique; C2 bans unmotivated glassmorphism — reconcile by permitting transparency only in non-content atmospheric background layers, never as default card surface treatment. (2) Skill prescribes 'dramatic shadows'; C4 bans the same soft shadow everywhere — compatible if dramatic means distinct and single-role (hard-offset or one floating element), which C4's uniformity check already passes. (3) Skill prescribes 'gradient meshes'; the C1 static check (src/audit.js checkPurpleGradient) only flags linear-gradient stops with hue 230–300 and sat >= 0.25, and does not match radial-gradient at all, so non-purple meshes pass the machine lane — but the LLM lane should still confirm the mesh serves the tone. (4) Philosophical conflict: design-tells encodes restraint as fixed rules (C3 single accent, C4 one shadow role), while the skill treats 'maximalist chaos' as an equally valid pole; a genuinely maximalist build could legitimately trip C3/C4 heuristics. design-interview should either make some audit rules tone-conditional or accept that its product is restrained/editorial by design and exclude maximalist tones from the menu. (5) Gap, not conflict: the skill's motion guidance (orchestrated load reveal, surprising hovers, CSS-only) has zero coverage in design-tells or the audit — a candidate positive lane. CAVEAT: the skill is direction-level prose with almost no numbers; every numeric threshold in the machineCheckable/buildApplication fields above is our operationalization, marked as 'suggested', not sourced. License is Apache-2.0 per the plugin's LICENSE file; ideas extracted, no prose copied.
